sanskrit_iast:
avyakto 'yam acintyo 'yam avikāryo 'yam ucyate |
tasmād evaṁ viditvainaṁ nānuśocitum arhasi || 25 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (transliteration body identical; Mukundananda house-style aspirate marks).
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs).

anvaya.meaning: unmanifest / not perceptible to the senses
unthinkable / not graspable by the intellect
you do not deserve / it is not fitting for you

translations.verbatim_quote: It is said that the soul is invisible, inconceivable and immutable. Knowing this, you should not grieve for the body.
The soul is spoken of as invisible, inconceivable, and unchangeable. Knowing this, you should not grieve for the body.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: As described previously, the magnitude of the soul is so small for our material calculation that he cannot be seen even by the most powerful microscope; therefore, he is invisible. As far as the soul's existence is concerned, no one can establish his existence experimentally beyond the proof of śruti, or Vedic wisdom.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Moreover, ucyate, it is said that; ayam, This, the Self; is avyaktah, unmanifest, since, being beyond the ken of all the organs, It cannot be objectified. For this very reason, ayam, This; is acintyah, inconceivable. For anything that comes within the purview of the organs becomes the object of thought.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the three properties as a chain. The Self is avyakta (unmanifest) because it is beyond the senses' reach. Being beyond the senses, it is therefore acintya (inconceivable) — what cannot be sensed cannot be objectified by thought. Being beyond both senses and thought, it is avikārya (unchangeable) — change requires being an object that can be acted upon, and the Self is no such object. The therefore-clause (tasmāt evaṁ viditvā) closes the imperishable block: knowing these three properties, grief over the body's fall is itself misplaced.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The self is not made manifest by those Pramanas (means of knowledge) by which objects susceptible of being cleft etc., are made manifest; hence It is unmanifest, being different in kind from objects susceptible to cleaving etc., It is inconceivable, being different in kind from all objects.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the three properties as kind-distinctions. The Self is unmanifest because it is different in kind from the objects that pramāṇas (means of knowledge — perception, inference) ordinarily manifest. It is inconceivable because it is different in kind from all objects that thought ordinarily handles. It is unchangeable because change is a property of objects within the empirical world, and the Self is not such an object. The therefore-clause closes the imperishable doctrine: grief, which has its locus in the empirical world, has no purchase on what is different in kind from that world.

positions.rendering: Knowing this, you should not grieve for the body
Knowing this, you should not grieve for the body
the closing 'therefore' completes the imperishable block (2.20-2.25); grief is misplaced because it locates the loss at the body, while what is real is the Self
grief, having its locus in the empirical world, has no purchase on what is different in kind from that world

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The translations correctly add 'for the body' to clarify what one is not to grieve, but neither original Sanskrit phrase says 'for the body' explicitly — both Shankara and Ramanuja read the verse with that addition implicit. The translations diverge from the commentaries in flattening the doctrinal logic: Shankara reads the three properties as a deductive chain (unmanifest → inconceivable → unchangeable) terminating in the no-grief instruction; Ramanuja reads the three as kind-distinctions establishing categorial separation between Self and the world where grief has its purchase. The translations preserve the conclusion but lose the argument structure that makes the conclusion follow.

literal_meaning: This (Self) is said to be unmanifest, unthinkable, and unchangeable. Therefore, knowing this thus, you should not grieve.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The closing verse of the imperishable block (2.20-2.25). Krishna names three final properties — avyakta (unmanifest, beyond the senses), acintya (unthinkable, beyond the intellect), avikārya (unchangeable, beyond modification) — and draws the practical conclusion: tasmād evaṁ viditvā nānuśocitum arhasi, 'therefore, knowing this thus, you should not grieve.' The verse functions as the doctrinal close-bracket for the entire imperishable argument running from 2.20. Shankara reads the three properties as a deductive chain (beyond senses → beyond thought → beyond change) and the therefore-clause as completing the syllogism. Ramanuja reads the three as kind-distinctions establishing that the Self is categorially separate from the world where grief operates. Both agree the verse's payload is the no-grief instruction, grounded in the imperishable's properties.
